topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The final notice, stark white against the faded beige of the administrative office, felt like a physical blow. Liam stared at the printed letter, his hand trembling so badly he had to grip the edge of the desk to steady it. Every word—*permanent closure, effective next semester*—seemed to echo in the sudden, profound silence of the room. He traced the embossed seal with a fingertip, the texture cool and unforgiving. He could feel a strange, heavy sinking in his chest, a pressure that made drawing a full breath a conscious effort. He didn't move to look at the window, where the autumn leaves were turning a brilliant, indifferent red; his focus was entirely trapped in the paper, the realization that the vibrant future he had meticulously mapped out was dissolving into dust. He finally let his head drop onto the cool surface of the desk, a quiet, shaky exhale escaping him.

[story 2]

"So, that's it then?" Maya asked, her voice thin, barely audible over the hum of the fluorescent lights. She kept re-reading the email, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. She pushed her chair back with a sharp scrape that seemed too loud in the cavernous lecture hall. Her palms were slick, and she found herself rubbing them together repeatedly, a nervous, repetitive motion. Looking around at the rows of empty seats, at the ghosts of countless late-night study sessions, a tightness constricted her throat. She felt a sudden, sharp sting behind her eyes, a desperate urge to wipe away the moisture, but she fought it back, swallowing the lump in her throat instead. "I just... I don't know what to do now," she managed, the admission tasting like ash.

[story 3]

The campus grounds seemed muted, the usual buzz of student life replaced by an eerie quiet. Ethan walked along the familiar path to the library, but his steps felt disconnected, heavy. He kept glancing at the imposing brick façade of the main academic building, a place that had been the backdrop to so many of his most significant memories. When he finally reached the entrance, he stopped, his shoulders slumping inward as if an invisible weight had settled upon him. He didn't bother entering; instead, he leaned against a cold stone pillar, closing his eyes tightly. A slow, hot trickle of tears escaped, tracing a path down his cheek, quickly followed by a desperate attempt to blink them away. The world felt tilted, as if the foundation beneath everything he knew had just crumbled away.
